title: Batch vs. Real-Time Predictions
concept: Batch predictions run offline on a schedule for many records at once, while real-time predictions respond instantly to a single request the moment it arrives.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does an app need to know to use a model's API?

options.text: Just the input/output contract — not the model's internal algorithm or features.
The model's complete internal source code.
The programming language the model was trained in.

exercises.explain: Correct! APIs hide internal complexity behind a simple input/output contract.

cards.title: Mail delivery vs. a live phone call
cards.body: Some communication is delivered on a schedule, like your mail carrier dropping off letters once a day. Other communication demands an instant answer, like a live phone call where you respond the moment the question is asked. Model predictions can be served in these same two styles.

cards.title: Batch vs. Real-Time Predictions
cards.body: Batch predictions run offline on a schedule, processing many records at once (e.g. nightly). Real-time predictions respond instantly to a single incoming request, the moment it's asked for.

cards.title: Newsletter vs. search autocomplete
cards.body: A batch prediction is like a nightly email newsletter — generated once for everyone, delivered on a fixed schedule. A real-time prediction is like a search engine's autocomplete — it must respond within milliseconds the instant you start typing, or the feature feels broken.

cards.title: Choosing the right style
cards.body: An e-commerce site might run a **batch** job overnight to compute 'recommended for you' emails for all 2 million users at once. The same site needs **real-time** predictions for fraud checks during checkout, since it must approve or block each transaction within a second.

cards.title: Real-time isn't always the 'better' choice
cards.body: You might assume real-time prediction is always superior since it's instant. In reality, batch processing is often far cheaper and more efficient for tasks that don't need instant results, since you can process millions of records together instead of handling each one individually.

cards.title: Batch vs. Real-Time
cards.body: Two prediction serving styles for different needs:

left.text:
Runs on a fixed schedule.
Processes many records at once.
e.g. nightly recommendation emails.

right.text:
Responds instantly per request.
One record at a time.
e.g. live fraud checkout checks.

options.text: Running predictions offline on a fixed schedule, processing many records together.
Responding instantly to a single request the moment it arrives.
A method for saving a model to disk.
A way to permanently delete old model versions.

exercises.explain: Correct! Batch predictions run offline on a schedule, processing large groups of records at once.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Batch prediction is always the worse choice, and real-time prediction should be used for every single use case.
exercises.explain: False! Batch processing is often cheaper and more efficient for tasks that don't require instant results.

exercises.sentence: ___ predictions respond instantly to a single request the moment it arrives.
exercises.explain: Real-time predictions must respond immediately, unlike batch jobs that run on a schedule.

exercises.prompt: A bank needs to instantly approve or decline a credit card swipe within one second at checkout. Which prediction style fits this need?

options.text: Real-time predictions, since the decision must happen instantly the moment the transaction occurs.
Batch predictions, since it can wait until the nightly processing job runs.
Neither style applies to fraud detection.

exercises.explain: Correct! An instant checkout decision absolutely requires real-time predictions, not a scheduled batch job.

exercises.prompt: A streaming service needs two things: 1) generate 'recommended shows' rows for all 10 million users to display tomorrow morning, and 2) instantly decide whether to show a 'continue watching' prompt the second a user opens the app. Which prediction styles fit each need?

options.text: Batch predictions for the overnight recommendation generation (processing millions at once), and real-time predictions for the instant app-open decision.
Real-time predictions for both tasks, since real-time is always strictly better.
Batch predictions for both tasks, since batch is always strictly cheaper.
Neither style is appropriate for a streaming service.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Bulk overnight processing for millions of users is a textbook batch job, while an instant per-user decision on app open needs real-time serving. Next, let's dig into what actually determines how 'instant' real-time responses can be: latency!
